critic_raw:
This RNN versus logistic regression readmission design is transparent about its limitations but has specific structural problems in the baseline comparison and sequence encoding that merit critique.

The logistic regression baseline is described as 'intentionally simpler' than the RNN's sequential input, explicitly using count aggregates (prior visit count, distinct diagnosis count, emergency visit count) while the RNN uses full visit sequences. The design states this difference 'is expected and part of the hypothesis.' This conflates the question 'does sequential modeling help?' with 'does having more granular features help?'. A logistic regression or gradient boosted model trained on the same granular features (event counts per visit, per-diagnosis history flags) would isolate architecture from feature richness. Without this intermediate baseline, any RNN advantage is uninterpretable.

The ICD-10 code vocabulary is collapsed to the top-50 diagnoses with a catch-all 'other' category for the RNN, and the binary indicators for 'high-risk diagnoses (CHF, COPD, diabetes)' are included in the logistic regression baseline. This means the RNN's sequence vector includes a rich top-50 diagnosis encoding while the logistic regression has only 3 binary disease flags. The RNN therefore has access to a substantially richer diagnostic vocabulary, not just sequential structure.

The training set covers months 1–12 and the test set months 19–24. With a 24-month dataset from a single hospital, any hospital-level intervention, policy change, or seasonal effect over 24 months (e.g., change in discharge criteria, COVID-related practice changes if the period includes 2020) will be conflated with model performance differences. The 6-month gap between training end and test start (months 13–18 are validation) adds a further potential distribution shift. The design acknowledges temporal drift but treats it as a confound to 'report' rather than actively control for.

The RNN sequence padding is to the 95th percentile length, with sequences truncated from one end (the design does not specify which end). If sequences are truncated from the beginning (oldest visits dropped), clinically important long-term history for chronically ill patients is systematically removed. If truncated from the end (most recent visits dropped), the model loses the clinically most relevant recent events. Neither is addressed.
all_issues_raised: The logistic regression baseline uses aggregated count features while the RNN uses full granular event sequences; this is a simultaneous change in architecture and feature richness, making any performance difference unattributable to sequential modeling capacity rather than richer feature representations.

The ICD-10 vocabulary is top-50 for the RNN but only 3 binary disease flags for the logistic regression, giving the RNN access to a much richer diagnostic encoding that conflates feature information content with sequential architecture in the comparison.

The 24-month single-hospital dataset is susceptible to hospital-level policy changes and seasonal effects (including COVID-period disruptions if applicable) that will differentially affect the 12-month training window versus the months 19-24 test window, confounding temporal generalization with distribution shift from operational changes.

The RNN's sequence truncation strategy (to 95th percentile length) is unspecified regarding which end is truncated; truncating from the beginning drops long-term disease history for chronic patients while truncating from the end drops the most recent pre-admission events, with materially different implications for what the model can learn.
all_issues_adjudicated: The logistic regression baseline uses aggregated count features while the RNN uses full granular event sequences; this is a simultaneous change in architecture and feature richness, making any performance difference unattributable to sequential modeling capacity rather than richer feature representations.

The ICD-10 vocabulary is top-50 for the RNN but only 3 binary disease flags for the logistic regression, giving the RNN access to a much richer diagnostic encoding that conflates feature information content with sequential architecture in the comparison.

The 24-month single-hospital dataset is susceptible to hospital-level policy changes and seasonal effects (including COVID-period disruptions if applicable) that will differentially affect the 12-month training window versus the months 19-24 test window, confounding temporal generalization with distribution shift from operational changes.
